title: Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT)

theory.introduction: Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT) is the quantum analogue of the classical discrete Fourier transform. It transforms a quantum state into a phase-based representation, enabling efficient solutions to problems involving periodicity, frequency estimation, and hidden structure in data.
key_points: QFT transforms amplitudes into phase information
QFT operates on quantum superposition states
It is exponentially faster than classical Fourier Transform for certain problems
QFT is a core component of Phase Estimation and Shor's Algorithm
QFT can be efficiently implemented using Hadamard and controlled phase gates

mathematical_representation.definition: For an n-qubit system, QFT transforms basis state |x⟩ as:
mathematical_representation.phase_encoding: Amplitude information is encoded into relative phase differences between basis states

intuition.description: QFT extracts periodic structure from quantum states. Instead of analyzing signals directly, QFT encodes frequency information into quantum phases.

circuit_structure.description: QFT is implemented using a sequence of Hadamard gates and controlled phase rotations.
building_blocks: Controlled phase gates introduce relative phase shifts

step_by_step.description: Apply Hadamard gate to first qubit
Apply controlled phase rotations with increasing precision
Reverse qubit order using swap gates

visualization.phase_representation: QFT converts amplitude differences into phase differences between basis states.
visualization.bloch_note: Bloch sphere visualization becomes insufficient for more than one qubit, but phase relationships can still be observed through simulator probability distributions.

summary: Quantum Fourier Transform efficiently converts quantum states into phase space representation. It enables exponential speedups in algorithms involving periodicity and eigenvalue estimation.

try_this: Observe how the Hadamard gates create superposition in both qubits
The CNOT introduces the phase relationship required for QFT
Compare amplitudes to see the phase encoding in action

quiz.question: What is the classical complexity of the Fast Fourier Transform on N points?
quiz.explanation: The classical FFT requires O(N log N) operations.

quiz.question: What is the quantum complexity of the Quantum Fourier Transform?
quiz.explanation: QFT requires only O((log N)^2) gate operations, an exponential improvement over classical FFT.

quiz.question: Which gate is the primary building block of QFT?
quiz.explanation: The Hadamard gate combined with controlled phase rotations forms the core of the QFT circuit.

quiz.question: What does QFT encode amplitude information into?
quiz.explanation: QFT transforms amplitude information into phase differences between basis states.

quiz.question: Which major algorithm uses QFT as a subroutine?
quiz.explanation: Shor's algorithm uses QFT for period finding, which enables integer factorization.
